steps.title: Apply the new function definition
steps.content: The vertex of an upward parabola lies midway: $$x = \tfrac{44+46}{2} = 45.$$

steps.title: Compute the answer
steps.content: So the minimum occurs at $x=45$. That matches choice B — B is correct.

steps.title: Use the source rationale
steps.content: Choice B is correct. It's given that $f\left(x\right)=\left(x-44\right)\left(x-46\right)$, which can be rewritten as $f\left(x\right)={x}^{2}-90x+2,024$. Since the coefficient of the ${x}^{2}$-term is positive, the graph of $y=f\left(x\right)$ in the xy-plane opens upward and reaches its minimum value at its vertex. For an equation in the form $f\left(x\right)=a{x}^{2}+bx+c$, where $a$, $b$, and $c$ are constants, the x-coordinate of the vertex is $-\frac{b}{2a}$. For the equation $f\left(x\right)={x}^{2}-90x+2,024$, $a=1$, $b=-90$, and $c=2,024$. It follows that the x-coordinate of the vertex is $-\frac{\left(-90\right)}{2\left(1\right)}$, or $45$. Therefore, $f\left(x\right)$ reaches its minimum when the value of $x$ is $45$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice A is incorrect. This is one of the x-coordinates of the x-intercepts of the graph of $y=f\left(x\right)$ in the xy-plane.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice C is incorrect. This is one of the x-coordinates of the x-intercepts of the graph of $y=f\left(x\right)$ in the xy-plane.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice D is incorrect. This is the y-coordinate of the vertex of the graph of $y=f\left(x\right)$ in the xy-plane.
